Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine at all. I'd try to study when I felt like it, but that wasn't often because I just couldn't find the motivation. Sometimes I'd sit down with my laptop intending to study for hours, but I'd end up scrolling through other things or just staring at the screen. My study time was scattered and inconsistent, usually happening late at night when anxiety about falling behind would hit me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly panic when I realized a deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd have work shifts or club activities that took priority, and studying just got pushed aside. There was no regularity to it—I was basically reacting to whatever felt most urgent in the moment.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about a deadline or boredom that made me log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about an assignment due and that would finally push me to open the LMS. Other times I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't actually accomplish much. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because earning points felt like a small win.
Q4: For a while, the gamified elements made me want to check in more regularly. I liked seeing my progress on the task badges and it gave me a little dopamine hit. But that routine faded when the novelty wore off and my stress about other things piled up. It never became a truly stable habit—more like a temporary phase that felt good while it lasted.
Q5: The video content was actually helpful when I could focus on it. I'm a hands-on learner, so watching someone demonstrate concepts worked better for me than just reading. The gamified tasks were also good because they broke things into smaller chunks that felt less overwhelming. I appreciated anything that didn't require me to sit still and read for hours.
Q6: The discussion forums felt pointless to me most of the time. I'd post the minimum required but rarely read what others wrote because it felt like busy work. Long reading materials I'd often skip or just skim because I couldn't maintain focus. If something didn't have an immediate deadline or points attached, I probably ignored it.
Q7: If something had clear rewards—like points, badges, or contributing directly to my grade—I'd do it. Activities that felt like they'd actually help me pass the exam seemed worth the effort. But if it was just 'enrichment' or 'optional practice,' I couldn't convince myself to care when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're already drowning and barely completing required work, doing extra stuff feels impossible. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which sometimes was the only thing that got me moving. Optional ones just added to my guilt about not doing enough.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone and try to review them during my commute or breaks at work. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics when the course materials weren't clicking for me. Sometimes I'd study from notes I'd handwritten, which helped me remember things better since I learn by doing. None of that showed up in the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was working through problems on paper or rewatching downloaded videos multiple times. I'd also study with a friend from class at the library, going over concepts together. My LMS activity would show nothing during those hours even though I was genuinely trying to learn. It's frustrating because the system can't see that effort.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video once, then watch it three or four more times offline when I was trying to understand something. Same with PDFs—I'd print them out because I focus better on paper, then mark them up and study from the physical copies. The LMS only saw that one initial download, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed for me. I was working extra shifts because I needed money, my anxiety was through the roof, and I just couldn't focus on anything. I'd sit in front of my computer feeling paralyzed, knowing I should study but unable to start. The course felt impossible and I started skipping classes because I was too anxious about being behind.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Sometimes I'd log in but just couldn't process anything because my brain was fried. Competing responsibilities always won because they had immediate consequences—like losing my job if I didn't show up for shifts.
Q14: So many times. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd scroll through materials without actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I was physically present on the LMS but mentally checked out, just going through the motions. It was like my body was doing the activity but my brain had disconnected.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments constantly because they felt too overwhelming to start. The anxiety of knowing I was unprepared made me procrastinate even more—it was this terrible cycle. I knew they were important but the thought of starting made me feel sick, so I'd do easier tasks or nothing at all. By the time I finally attempted them, I was rushing and couldn't do my best work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then frantically try to complete things. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I could force myself to act. The LMS probably shows these huge spikes in my activity right before due dates, which is pretty accurate to how I operated.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, especially if it was positive. But negative feedback just made my anxiety worse and made me want to avoid the course entirely. I'd sometimes not even look at feedback because I was scared of what it would say. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal and judgmental.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many different components to track. Having nine online courses meant I was constantly juggling and nothing got my full attention. The weekly modules were helpful in theory, but I'd fall behind and then everything would pile up. More flexibility might have helped, or fewer but more focused assignments.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they gave me small wins when everything else felt like failure. But the sheer volume of content made it harder because I never felt like I could catch up. The attendance requirements were tough too since I missed a lot of classes due to work and anxiety. Shorter, more interactive activities would have worked better for how I learn.
Q20: That's literally me. I was on the LMS a lot because of the gamified stuff and because logging in felt productive even when it wasn't. But actually completing and submitting quality work was a different story—that required focus and energy I didn't have. Sometimes high activity is just someone desperately trying to engage but not being able to follow through.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone is really efficient or studies effectively offline. They might understand the material from one read-through or have good notes from class. For me, it was the opposite—I had high activity but still bombed the exam because clicking around isn't the same as deep learning. But yeah, activity levels don't tell the whole story either way.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which shows some level of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you were actually learning or just present. They can't capture offline study, mental state, or real understanding. A log might show I watched a video, but it can't show that I was crying from stress while it played.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material. It's when your brain is active and you're building understanding, not just completing tasks to check boxes. For me, true engagement was rare this semester—I was mostly just surviving and going through motions. Real engagement requires mental space and motivation that I struggled to find.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting just to show up at all. It can't capture the panic attacks before logging in, or the tears when I realized I was failing. It doesn't know about my work schedule, my financial stress, or how my anxiety made everything ten times harder. My effort was real even if my results didn't show it—that's something data will never capture.
